definition: In spite of what has just been said or referred to
example: The brute force works. Nevertheless, it fails at scale.

definition: As a result of something that has just been stated
example: The input was unsorted; consequently, binary search failed.

definition: In addition to what has already been said
example: The algorithm is efficient. Furthermore, it is easy to implement.

definition: Used to show a clear difference between two things
example: Merge sort is stable. In contrast, quicksort is not.

definition: Because of the thing just mentioned
example: We used memoization; as a result, time complexity dropped to O(n).

definition: Used to make a comparison with something different
example: DFS uses a stack. By contrast, BFS uses a queue.

definition: Used to summarize the fundamental nature of something
example: In essence, dynamic programming avoids redundant computation.

definition: Used to intensify a denial of what has just been said
example: It is not slow. On the contrary, it runs in O(log n) time.

definition: Used to add another point to what has been said
example: The function handles nulls. In addition, it validates types.

definition: As a result of an action or condition
example: The loop runs n times; as a consequence, time complexity is O(n).

definition: Used to introduce a statement that contrasts with what has just been said
example: It is fast; however, it uses a lot of memory.

definition: In spite of the fact that; even though
example: Although the array is sorted, we must still check for duplicates.

definition: Despite what has just been said
example: The cache is small. Even so, it improves performance significantly.

definition: Used to introduce a contrasting point, often a caveat
example: The system is robust. That said, it cannot handle infinite loops.

definition: But at the same time; nevertheless
example: It is a simple design, yet highly effective.

definition: During the time that; whereas (introducing a contrast)
example: While arrays have fixed sizes, linked lists are dynamic.

definition: As a further matter; besides
example: The solution is elegant. Moreover, it is extremely readable.

definition: In addition to; apart from
example: Besides edge cases, we also need to test concurrent requests.

definition: In a similar way
example: Trees use pointers. Similarly, graphs rely on node references.

definition: For that reason; consequently
example: The user is not authenticated. Therefore, we return a 401.

definition: As a result or consequence of this
example: We drop the constants, thus arriving at O(n).

definition: As a consequence; for this reason
example: The data is immutable; hence, thread safety is guaranteed.

definition: Because of or on account of
example: The delay was owing to database locks.

definition: Caused by or ascribable to
example: The crash was due to a null pointer exception.

definition: After a particular thing has happened; afterward
example: We parse the JSON, and subsequently map it to objects.

definition: At first
example: Initially, I considered a hash map, but an array is better here.

definition: To give a brief statement of the main points
example: To summarise, the time complexity is linear and space is constant.

definition: To state something concisely
example: In short, we trade memory for speed.

definition: Taken as a whole; in all
example: Overall, this architecture handles scale beautifully.

definition: As an example
example: Many components are reusable; for instance, the button component.
